grammar.title: Direct address + imperative
grammar.detail: ‘Amos, proporcionen…’ — the vocative ‘Amos’ (Masters) sets the audience; ‘proporcionen’ is the ustedes-imperative addressed to them.

grammar.title: ‘conscientes de que…’
grammar.detail: Absolute construction — a participial phrase set off by commas, equivalent to ‘mindful that…’. Common in Spanish moral exhortation.

expression.phrase: ustedes también tienen un Amo en el cielo
expression.note: Beautiful wordplay — the same word ‘amo’ for both an earthly master and the heavenly Master. The accountability runs upward.

grammar.title: Reflexive imperative ‘Dedíquense’
grammar.detail: ustedes-imperative of dedicarse. Pronoun attaches to the end; the resulting form takes an accent on the antepenult.

grammar.title: ‘perseveren en ella’
grammar.detail: ‘ella’ refers back to ‘la oración’ — feminine, so the pronoun is feminine. Without context, Spanish pronouns carry the gender of their antecedent.

expression.phrase: Dedíquense a la oración: perseveren en ella con agradecimiento
expression.note: Three ingredients of prayer in one verse — devotion, perseverance, thankfulness. The colon marks the unpacking of the first command.

grammar.title: ‘a fin de que’ + subjunctive
grammar.detail: Purpose clause — always subjunctive. ‘a fin de que Dios nos abra’ → ‘abra’ = present subjunctive of abrir.

grammar.title: ‘al mismo tiempo’
grammar.detail: ‘at the same time’ — adverbial phrase used to connect simultaneous actions.

expression.phrase: que Dios nos abra las puertas para proclamar la palabra
expression.note: The ‘open door’ metaphor (cf. 1 Cor 16:9, 2 Cor 2:12) — opportunity for the gospel, even from prison. Paul asks others to pray for what he wants most: more chances to speak.
